front: Figure: China's reformist "paramount leader" from 1978 to 1992, though he never formally held the title of General Secretary or head of state
Figure: outmaneuvered Hua Guofeng, Mao Zedong's chosen successor, in a power struggle to take power
Figure: was denounced during the Cultural Revolution as the "Number 2 capitalist roader"
Figure: resigned in the wake of the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests and was succeeded by Jiang Zemin
Figure: Susan Shirk's book Overreach examines the "ghost" he left behind after the end of his "hide and bide" foreign policy doctrine
Figure: was blocked by the Gang of Four from succeeding Zhou Enlai as Premier
work: Rise to Power: Cultural Revolution & Purges

front: Figure: was placed under house arrest in 1976 after being accused of planning a Tiananmen Square protest triggered by the removal of displays of mourning [for Zhou Enlai]
work: Rise to Power: Cultural Revolution & Purges

front: Figure: served as propaganda director of the Jiangxi Soviet early in his career, and stated that Mao Zedong was "70% right and 30% wrong"
work: Rise to Power: Cultural Revolution & Purges

front: Figure: as chairman of the Central Military Commission, reopened China's universities but ordered a crackdown on the Democracy Wall movement
work: Rise to Power: Cultural Revolution & Purges

front: Figure: established China's first Special Economic Zones (SEZs), including Shenzhen, to encourage export-led development
Figure: famously argued it didn't matter whether "a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice," as an analogy for pragmatic economic policy
Figure: introduced the Household Responsibility System, a rural contracting policy that helped revive Township and Village Enterprises
Figure: his liberalizing economic reforms allowed Western chains like KFC and McDonald's to open franchises in China
Figure: declared that "to be rich is glorious"
Figure: formulated the "One Country, Two Systems" principle, allowing regions like Hong Kong and Macau to retain capitalist systems under Chinese sovereignty
Figure: instituted the one-child policy
Figure: enacted Zhou Enlai's Four Modernizations program
Figure: presided over the resumption of Confucian rituals in 1984, as part of China's transition to a market economy
Figure: undertook a "Southern Tour" in 1992 to publicize the success of his economic reforms, touring the Special Economic Zones he had created
Figure: publicly supported the nascent Shenzhen and Shanghai stock exchanges during his 1992 tour of southern China
Figure: launched an invasion of Vietnam in 1979; the following year, sent his successor to study Ireland's Shannon Airport
work: Foreign Policy: the Sino-Vietnamese War and the June 9 Speech

front: Figure: defended the Tiananmen Square crackdown in a June 9, 1989 speech, citing the Kent State shootings as proof the U.S. lacked the moral authority to criticize China
work: Foreign Policy: the Sino-Vietnamese War and the June 9 Speech

front: Figure: sued astrophysicist and dissident Fang Lizhi for libel
work: Foreign Policy: the Sino-Vietnamese War and the June 9 Speech

front: Figure: as de facto leader, ordered the military crackdown on the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, memorialized by the "Tank Man" photograph
Figure: reportedly went for a swim in his pool after suppressing the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, echoing Mao Zedong's own cult-of-personality swims
Figure: his son became paraplegic after jumping or being thrown from a window during a Cultural Revolution torture session, and later campaigned for disabled persons' rights
Figure: hearing loss forced him to work from a home office on Kuang Street
Figure: wore a cowboy hat during a 1979 state visit to the United States, a moment highlighted in biographies by Richard Evans and Ezra Vogel
